mainmatter.p: To show that \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( I , A \right ) \mathclose {}} is contractible, we choose \alpha _{A} : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( I , A \right ) \mathclose {}} as the center of contraction. To identify any f : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( I , A \right ) \mathclose {}} with \alpha _{A}, we use the wild naturality of { \Delta I } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ 1 _{ \mathcal {C} } }. In particular, we have an identification \alpha _f between \alpha _I;f and 1 _{ I } ; \alpha _A. By our assumption that \alpha _I= 1 _{ I } and using the wild left-unit identifications \lambda f and \lambda \alpha _A, we may identify f with \alpha _A.

frontmatter.title: Wild initial objects from universal cones

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {C} be a wild category, and let I: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} } be an object of \mathcal {C}. If we may equip a wild natural transformation { \Delta I } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ 1 _{ \mathcal {C} } } from the constant 0-functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ \Delta I }}{ \mathcal {C} } to the identity 0-functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ 1 _{ \mathcal {C} } }}{ \mathcal {C} } with an identification c_ \alpha : \alpha _I = 1 _{ I }, then \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( I , A \right ) \mathclose {}} is contractible for all A: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} }.

(Our proof will follow that of the nLab, which was stated for ordinary categories and functors and natural transformations rather than wild ones. It was Mike Shulman who noticed who noticed that this proof also works for the wild/incoherent notions studied here.)
A wild category is given by the following data:

ol.li: for each A,B: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} }, a family of types \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}};
for each A: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} }, an element 1 _{ A } : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , A \right ) \mathclose {}};

for each f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} and g: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( B , C \right ) \mathclose {}}, an element { \mathopen {} \left ( f;g \right ) \mathclose {}} : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , C \right ) \mathclose {}}

for each f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}}, a identification \mathsf { lunit } _{ \mathcal {C} } f : { \mathopen {} \left ( 1 _{ A } ;f \right ) \mathclose {}} = f and a identification \mathsf { runit } _{ \mathcal {C} } f : { \mathopen {} \left ( f; 1 _{ B } \right ) \mathclose {}} =f;

for each f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} and g: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( B , C \right ) \mathclose {}} and h: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( C , D \right ) \mathclose {}}, a identification \mathsf { assoc } _{ \mathcal {C} } \, f \, g \, h : f; { \mathopen {} \left ( g;h \right ) \mathclose {}} = { \mathopen {} \left ( f;g \right ) \mathclose {}} ;h.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {C} , \mathcal {D} be two wild categories, and let F,G: \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {D} be two wild 0-functors from \mathcal {C} to \mathcal {D}. A wild natural transformation is defined by the following data:

ol.li: for each A: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} }, an element \alpha _{A} : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathbf {ob} _{ F } A , \mathbf {ob} _{ G } A \right ) \mathclose {}};

for each f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}}, an identification \alpha _{f} : \alpha _A; \mathbf {hom} _{ G } {f} = \mathbf {hom} _{ F } {f}; \alpha _B.
